title: AI Capability Stratification
subtitle: A Framework for the Future Distribution of AI Power

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan
creators.affiliation: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

publisher_unit: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center

alternate_identifiers.description: Policy brief number printed in the PDF

alternate_identifiers.identifier: AI Capability Stratification A Framework for the Future Distribution of AI Power.pdf

alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title
Visible public-facing layer of AI capability in the framework
Permissioned, professional, organizational, and embedded domain of AI capability
Restricted-visibility, high-threshold, high-resource, and high-consequence domain of AI capability
Seven-layer stratification model developed in the policy brief

description: This policy brief proposes AI Capability Stratification as a structural framework for understanding the future distribution of AI power. It argues that public-facing AI represents only the visible surface of a broader capability structure, while deeper forms of AI power emerge through institutional embedding, proprietary knowledge, operational control, resource authority, real-world interfaces, and differentiated rule conditions.
abstract: The future development of artificial intelligence should not be understood as a linear race among public-facing large models. As AI systems become embedded in institutions, workflows, operational systems, proprietary knowledge environments, resource-allocation mechanisms, and differentiated rule conditions, AI capability is likely to form a stratified structure. This policy brief proposes AI Capability Stratification as a structural framework for understanding the future distribution of artificial intelligence power. The framework shifts attention from model performance alone to the broader conditions under which AI capability is accessed, embedded, authorized, optimized, and connected to real-world systems. The model divides AI capability into three broad domains: Surface Intelligence Domain, Deep Intelligence Domain, and Dark-Domain Intelligence. These domains can be further divided into seven analytical layers: Public Interface Intelligence, Institutional Embedded Intelligence, Operational Control Intelligence, Proprietary Epistemic Intelligence, Objective-Specific Optimized Intelligence, High-Authority Resource Intelligence, and Boundary-Condition Intelligence. The central thesis is straightforward: Public AI determines visible access; Deep AI determines professional productivity; and Dark-domain AI determines how AI capability is linked to concentrated authority, resource command, real-world consequence, and differentiated rule conditions. This framework does not claim that deeper AI layers are always more intelligent, more advanced, or more dangerous. Rather, it argues that AI power depends on the interaction between model capability, data quality, access conditions, institutional embedding, resource authority, real-world interfaces, and rule space. The future distribution of AI power will therefore not be determined only by which model is the strongest. It will also be determined by who can use AI, what AI can access, what resources AI can influence, what systems AI can affect, and under what rules AI is allowed to operate.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on Strategic Competition, AI-Enabled Warfare, and Information Conflict
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on strategic competition, AI-enabled warfare, information conflict, systemic risk, logistics resilience, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.source_basis: Conceptual policy analysis and structural analogy using the public web, deep web, and dark web as a non-moral stratification model, combined with prior EPINOVA work on AI power, foundation-model research, AI risk-management frameworks, frontier-AI governance literature, and public AI governance instruments including the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, OECD AI principles, and OMB federal AI governance guidance.
methodology.case_context: Future distribution of AI capability across public-facing systems, embedded organizational systems, proprietary and domain-specific AI, operational control systems, high-authority resource systems, and differentiated rule-space environments.
methodology.core_problem: The central analytical problem is that public-facing model performance does not capture the full distribution of AI power once AI systems become embedded in institutions, proprietary data environments, operational workflows, resource-allocation structures, real-world interfaces, and differentiated rule conditions.
central_claims: Public-facing AI is only the visible surface of future AI power.
AI capability will stratify across access, data, authority, specialization, interface, resource authority, and rule space.
Deep AI will become a primary layer of professional productivity through institutional embedding and proprietary knowledge systems.

Dark-domain AI should be understood structurally rather than morally, as a domain of restricted visibility, high access thresholds, concentrated authority, resource connection, real-world consequence, and differentiated rule conditions.

AGI would not eliminate stratification because general capability does not automatically provide proprietary data, institutional memory, expert feedback, resource authority, operational interfaces, or special permissions.
AI governance must move beyond public-model regulation to address embedded, operational, proprietary, high-authority, and boundary-condition AI systems.

analytical_lenses.name: AI–Internet Stratification Analogy
analytical_lenses.description: Uses the public web, deep web, and dark web analogy structurally to distinguish visibility, access, authority, specialization, operational connection, and rule conditions in AI systems.

analytical_lenses.name: Surface Intelligence Domain
analytical_lenses.description: Analyzes public-facing AI systems that shape mass adoption, public perception, and visible AI markets.

analytical_lenses.name: Deep Intelligence Domain
analytical_lenses.description: Analyzes permissioned, professional, organizational, and embedded AI systems tied to workflows, proprietary data, and institutional knowledge.

analytical_lenses.name: Dark-Domain Intelligence
analytical_lenses.description: Analyzes restricted-visibility, high-threshold, high-resource, high-consequence, and differentiated-rule AI systems without treating them as inherently illegal, malicious, or opaque.

analytical_lenses.name: Seven-Layer Capability Model
analytical_lenses.description: Classifies AI capability into Public Interface, Institutional Embedded, Operational Control, Proprietary Epistemic, Objective-Specific Optimized, High-Authority Resource, and Boundary-Condition Intelligence.

analytical_lenses.name: AGI Stratification Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Evaluates why general capability would not automatically erase stratification based on data, authority, interfaces, permissions, and rule conditions.

analytical_lenses.name: Layered Governance Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Links governance requirements to the structural position of AI systems across public, embedded, proprietary, high-authority, and boundary-condition environments.

dimensions.name: Audience Scale
dimensions.observed_pattern: AI systems vary from mass public access to narrow authorized communities, firms, agencies, military organizations, research institutions, or small expert groups.
dimensions.interpretation: Audience scale affects visibility, diffusion, public accountability, specialization, and institutional consequence.

dimensions.name: Access Threshold
dimensions.observed_pattern: Technical, institutional, financial, legal, or security barriers determine who can use an AI system.
dimensions.interpretation: Higher access thresholds may reduce visibility while increasing specialization, control, and consequence.

dimensions.name: Data Quality
dimensions.observed_pattern: Systems differ by the relevance, exclusivity, reliability, timeliness, and domain value of their data.
dimensions.interpretation: In many domains, data quality and exclusivity may matter more than model scale.

dimensions.name: Domain Specificity
dimensions.observed_pattern: AI systems may be general-purpose or optimized for a particular sector, function, environment, task, or decision problem.
dimensions.interpretation: Specialization can produce stronger task-level performance even when general model capability is lower.

dimensions.name: Resource Authority
dimensions.observed_pattern: AI systems may recommend, allocate, trigger, or command material, financial, computational, organizational, legal, administrative, or coercive resources.
dimensions.interpretation: Resource authority determines whether AI capability can move from assistance to consequential resource influence.

dimensions.name: Real-World Intervention Capacity
dimensions.observed_pattern: Some systems remain advisory while others can affect workflows, machines, markets, infrastructure, administrative systems, military systems, or external environments.
dimensions.interpretation: Intervention capacity is a major dividing line between informational AI and operational AI.

dimensions.name: Rule Space
dimensions.observed_pattern: Public, enterprise, defense, intelligence, emergency, research, and critical-infrastructure systems operate under different legal, regulatory, organizational, ethical, and exceptional conditions.
dimensions.interpretation: Rule space determines what forms of AI capability are enabled, limited, normalized, or restricted.

key_findings: Public AI is only the visible surface of future AI power.
AI capability is likely to stratify across access, data, authority, specialization, interface, resource authority, and rule space.
Surface Intelligence shapes public perception, consumer adoption, user expectations, and the visible AI market, but it does not represent the full structure of AI power.

Deep Intelligence is likely to become a primary site of AI value accumulation through institutional embedding, operational control, proprietary knowledge, expert feedback, and objective-specific optimization.
Dark-Domain Intelligence should be understood as a structural category rather than a moral label; it does not imply illegality, malicious intent, or inherent opacity.
The seven layers are not a simple intelligence hierarchy; depth changes the structure of capability rather than necessarily increasing intrinsic model intelligence.
AGI would not eliminate stratification because access to data, institutions, resource authority, interfaces, and special permissions would still remain unevenly distributed.
AI governance must move beyond public-facing models and address embedded, operational, proprietary, high-authority, and boundary-condition AI systems.
policy_recommendations: Distinguish model capability from system capability when evaluating AI power and risk.
Develop layered governance rather than one-size-fits-all rules for public-interface, deep, and dark-domain AI systems.
Assess AI systems by access, data, authority, resource connection, intervention capacity, and rule space, not only by benchmark performance or public model ranking.
Require documentation, internal review, sector-specific standards, independent audits, incident reporting, and clear institutional responsibility for proprietary and embedded AI systems.
Apply stronger authorization, accountability, oversight, escalation safeguards, and limits on resource-command authority for high-authority and boundary-condition AI systems.

Treat AGI governance as a stratification problem as well as a capability problem, because general intelligence would still be distributed through institutional embedding, data access, permissions, resource authority, and real-world interfaces.

watchpoints.indicator: Rapid deployment of AI systems inside enterprise workflows, government administration, finance, health care, infrastructure, or defense without comparable public visibility
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tracks the shift from public AI visibility to embedded AI consequence.

watchpoints.indicator: Increasing use of proprietary data, institutional memory, expert feedback, and internal workflow data as sources of AI advantage
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Identifies the growth of Proprietary Epistemic Intelligence and Deep Intelligence.

watchpoints.indicator: AI systems connected to resource allocation, infrastructure operations, logistics, procurement, emergency response, or security decisions
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Detects the expansion of Operational Control and High-Authority Resource Intelligence.

watchpoints.indicator: Different governance regimes emerging for consumer AI, enterprise AI, defense AI, critical-infrastructure AI, and frontier research systems
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tracks rule-space differentiation as a driver of AI capability stratification.

watchpoints.indicator: Public debate continuing to rely primarily on model rankings, benchmark performance, or public chatbot capability
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Identifies analytical blind spots where visible model performance is mistaken for total AI power.

watchpoints.indicator: AGI or frontier-model discussions assuming that general capability eliminates the importance of proprietary data, authorization, interfaces, and institutional embedding
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tests whether future AI analysis accounts for the persistence of stratification.

figures.title: AI Capability Stratification: Three Domains and Seven Layers
figures.description: Presents the AI Capability Stratification framework as an iceberg model, with Public Interface Intelligence at the visible surface and deeper layers embedded in institutions, operational systems, proprietary knowledge environments, high-authority resource structures, and differentiated rule conditions.
figures.note: The figure is conceptual and does not imply a linear ranking of intelligence. Depth represents structural position, including access threshold, data environment, institutional embedding, resource authority, real-world intervention capacity, and rule space, rather than intrinsic model capability. Dark-Domain Intelligence is used analytically to describe restricted visibility and differentiated operating conditions, not illegality, malicious use, or inherent opacity.

funding.statement: No external funding information is provided in the PDF.

software.statement: No software artifact is specified in the PDF.

disclaimer: This policy brief is an institutional publication of EPINOVA, prepared by Dr. Shaoyuan Wu in his capacity as Director of the Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC. The analysis is based on publicly available information and scenario-based analytical estimates and does not represent the official position of any government. The publication is intended solely for research and policy discussion purposes and does not constitute legal, military, operational, or sanctions-compliance advice.
